food_subgroup: Mixed protein bento with rice — grilled fish and fried chicken

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general bento food product class. No specific saba-and-chicken-karaage bento entry exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers composite bento-style meals. No exact code for saba-and-and-chicken-karaage combination; closest_parent applied.

allergens: wheat (potato starch may be substituted but soy marinade contains wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: One bento box typically contains approximately 450 g total, comprising a portion of grilled mackerel (about 80 g), chicken karaage pieces (about 100 g), and steamed white rice (about 200 g), with the remainder being accompaniments such as pickles or salad.

nutrient_source.title: Composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central: Atlantic mackerel cooked (FDC ID 175136), chicken thigh meat fried in batter (FDC ID 171477), and white rice cooked (FDC ID 168878); weighted by typical bento component proportions (mackerel 18%, chicken 22%, rice 44%, other 16%).
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific saba-and-chicken-karaage bento. Values are weighted composites from USDA FoodData Central using component proportions representative of a standard Japanese bento box: grilled mackerel 18 g per 100 g total, fried chicken 22 g per 100 g total, steamed white rice 44 g per 100 g total, and accompaniments (pickled vegetables, edamame) 16 g per 100 g total. Energy cross-checked against HPB Singapore nutrient data for analogous Japanese bento dishes.

gi_source.title: Analogous estimate based on: 'International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021' — white rice cooked GI 72 (medium-to-high), composite meal GI reduced by protein and fat co-ingestion; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific bento combination. White rice (cooked) has a GI of approximately 72 (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). Mixed meals containing protein (fish, chicken) and fat attenuate the glycemic response. A composite meal GI of approximately 54 is estimated, which falls into the 'low' GI category (GI <= 55). GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 86.4) / 100 ≈ 46.7, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 19.2 × 4.5 = 86.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full 450 g bento delivers approximately 1395 mg sodium, representing 58–70% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2400 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this bento to occasional consumption and request reduced-sodium soy sauce marinades, as the soy-based karaage marinade and grilled mackerel seasoning are the primary sodium contributors.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated composite GI of 54 (low) driven by white rice; however, a full serving delivers approximately 86 g of carbohydrate, resulting in a high glycemic load (46.7), which requires careful portion management for blood glucose control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consider requesting a smaller rice portion or substituting with brown rice to manage the high glycemic load, and should account for the approximately 86 g total carbohydrate per bento box when planning insulin or medication dosing.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Mackerel (saba) is rich in long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are cardioprotective; however, the deep-frying of chicken karaage adds saturated fat and the soy marinade contributes sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The omega-3 content from grilled mackerel is beneficial for cardiovascular health, but individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the total sodium load and the saturated fat from fried chicken, and should prefer this dish over higher-saturated-fat alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Saba and Chicken Karaage Bento (サバと鶏の唐揚げ弁当) is a Japanese-style bento box meal combining two distinct protein preparations: grilled mackerel (saba) and crispy deep-fried chicken karaage, served alongside a generous portion of steamed Japanese white rice. Saba (Atlantic or Pacific mackerel) is typically seasoned with salt and grilled until the skin is lightly charred and the flesh is moist and flaky, delivering a rich, savoury, slightly oily flavour characteristic of oily fish. Chicken karaage is prepared by marinating bite-sized chicken thigh pieces in soy sauce, ginger, garlic, and sake, coating them in potato starch or a starch-flour blend, and deep-frying until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining juicy inside. The bento format — a compartmentalised box — is a cornerstone of Japanese food culture, offering a balanced, portable meal. Nutritionally, this bento provides a meaningful source of complete protein from both fish and poultry, long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) from the mackerel, B vitamins including B12 and niacin, and selenium. The white rice component contributes the majority of carbohydrates and energy. The dish has a low glycemic index but carries a high glycemic load per serving due to the total carbohydrate content, and a moderate sodium level from soy-based marinades. It is suitable for most healthy adults as part of a varied diet, but individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease should monitor portion size and sodium intake. In Singapore, this dish is available at Japanese bento restaurants such as Shock Japanese Bento and is priced at approximately SGD 8.90.
common_user_queries: What is in a saba chicken karaage bento?
How many calories in a Japanese mackerel chicken bento?
Is chicken karaage bento good for diabetics?
How much sodium in a Japanese bento box?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific saba-and-chicken-karaage bento combination. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component data for grilled mackerel, fried chicken, and cooked white rice, weighted by representative bento proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on published mixed-meal GI attenuation data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are weighted composite estimates from USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) using component proportions: mackerel 18%, fried chicken 22%, steamed white rice 44%, accompaniments 16%. No HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific dish. 2. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this bento combination was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The estimate is based on white rice GI (72, Atkinson et al. 2021) attenuated by co-ingested protein and fat per published mixed-meal GI literature, resulting in a low GI category. 3. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and all B vitamins are estimated from USDA component data; these are not -1 sentinel values but estimated numeric values. 4. Halal status is marked false as standard karaage uses mirin and sake (alcohol-containing); halal-certified variants may exist but cannot be confirmed for this specific field dataing. 5. The input record listed cuisine_tags including Korean and Chinese alongside Japanese; these are delivery-platform categorisation tags and do not reflect the dish's culinary origin, which is Japanese. 6. Serving size of 450 g is a representative estimate for a standard Japanese bento box of this type; actual restaurant portion may vary. 7. All -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values derived from USDA component data; no -1 sentinel values remain in this record.
